papers.title: Lineage-aware stochastic modeling reveals gene-expression dynamics in development and disease.
papers.abstract: Gene expression evolves dynamically along cell lineages, yet most analysis methods treat single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) data as static snapshots and fail to exploit phylogenetic relationships among cells. Recent advances in cell-lineage tracing now enable the reconstruction of high-resolution lineage phylogenies, providing a natural framework for identifying when and where transcriptional changes arise during development, differentiation, and disease progression. Some models of gene expression have begun to consider phylogenetic structure, but they generally rely on imprecise Gaussian assumpt
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This paper introduces a new stochastic modeling framework that leverages cell lineage phylogenies to analyze gene expression dynamics in single-cell RNA-seq data, overcoming the limitations of static snapshot methods. By incorporating lineage relationships, the model more accurately identifies when and where transcriptional changes occur during development and disease progression. This approach improves upon previous phylogenetic models that relied on imprecise Gaussian assumptions.

papers.title: Spatial co-expression and cell-cell communication inference from spatially resolved transcriptomics with CONCISE.
papers.abstract: Cell-cell communication is fundamental to tissue organization, homeostasis, and disease progression. Recent advances in spatial transcriptomics provide unprecedented opportunities to systematically characterize ligand-receptor interactions directly within intact tissues. However, robust inference of spatial ligand-receptor interactions remains challenging because intrinsic features of spatial transcriptomics data, including spatial autocorrelation, variation in total molecular counts, and measurement errors, can induce spurious spatial co-expression and lead to inflated false-positive results.
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: CONCISE is a new computational method that accurately infers cell-cell communication from spatial transcriptomics data by accounting for spatial autocorrelation, molecular count variation, and measurement errors that can cause false positives. The approach improves the reliability of identifying ligand-receptor interactions directly within intact tissues, addressing a key challenge in the field.

papers.title: Spatial Transcriptomics Recontextualizes the Cellular Environment of Conjunctival Melanoma.
papers.abstract: Conjunctival melanoma (CM) is a rare cancer with a potentially high recurrence rate. The mechanics of its progression, its relationship with neighboring tissues, and its molecular characteristics are largely unknown. Diagnosis currently requires a biopsy and the time and expertise of a pathologist.
papers.journal: medRxiv : the preprint server for health sciences
papers.summary: This study uses spatial transcriptomics to map the cellular environment of conjunctival melanoma, revealing how the tumor interacts with surrounding tissues at the molecular level. The findings provide new insights into the progression of this rare cancer and may lead to improved diagnostic approaches.

papers.title: Spatial Transcriptomic Profiling Reveals Microenvironment-Dependent Immune Signatures in a Lyme Arthritis model.
papers.abstract: Lyme arthritis, a manifestation of Lyme disease, is triggered by the spirochetal bacterium Borrelia burgdorferi ( Bb ), which is transmitted through the bite of the Ixodes tick. Although multiple studies have been conducted on the complex host immune response in Lyme arthritis, the spatial gene expression environment in the joint tissue remains unexplored. Here, we applied spatial transcriptomics to ankle joints of C3H mice infected with Bb , profiling tissues at peak inflammation (two weeks post infection) and after antibiotics (four weeks post-infection) during inflammation resolution. Analy
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study used spatial transcriptomics to map gene expression in mouse ankle joints during Lyme arthritis, revealing how the local tissue environment shapes immune responses at peak inflammation and after antibiotic treatment. The findings provide the first spatial view of host immune dynamics in Lyme arthritis, highlighting microenvironment-dependent immune signatures.

papers.title: TIDEST: post-imputation differential expression testing for spatial transcriptomics data.
papers.abstract: Spatial transcriptomics enables the study of tissue organization in situ , but many high-resolution platforms measure only a limited gene panel, leaving much of the transcriptome unobserved. Although deep learning methods can reconstruct missing genes from matched single-cell references, downstream differential expression (DE) analysis remains unreliable because prediction uncertainty and spatially structured sources of variation are typically ignored. These factors can bias effect estimates and inflate false discoveries. We present TIDEST , a framework for DE testing after spatial transcripto
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: TIDEST is a new statistical framework for performing differential expression analysis on spatial transcriptomics data after imputation of missing genes. It addresses the problem that current methods ignore prediction uncertainty and spatial structure, which can lead to biased results and false discoveries. This work provides a more reliable approach for downstream analysis of imputed spatial transcriptomics data.

papers.title: pyVIPER: a fast and scalable Python package for protein activity estimation and master regulator analysis of single-cell RNA sequencing data.
papers.abstract: Single-cell sequencing has revolutionized biomedical research by offering insights into cellular heterogeneity at unprecedented resolution. Yet, the low signal-to-noise ratio characteristic of single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) challenges quantitative analyses. Gene regulatory network (GRN) analysis can help overcome this obstacle, enabling the mechanistic elucidation of cellular state determinants. For instance, the VIPER algorithm can identify Master Regulator proteins from gene expression data. However, as the size and complexity of scRNA-seq datasets grow, the demand for scalable tools
papers.summary: pyVIPER is a new Python package that enables fast and scalable estimation of protein activity and identification of master regulator proteins from single-cell RNA sequencing data. It addresses the computational challenges posed by large scRNA-seq datasets while leveraging gene regulatory network analysis to overcome the low signal-to-noise ratio inherent in such data.

papers.title: Spatially informed reference-free cell-type deconvolution for spatial transcriptomics with SpatialCD.
papers.abstract: Cell-type deconvolution has been instrumental for the analysis of spatial transcriptomics (ST) data to reveal underlying tissue heterogeneity. Although reference-based methods have been widely explored, practical limitations, particularly the need for matched single-cell RNA-seq data sets, highlight the value of robust reference-free methods. Existing reference-free approaches, such as STdeconvolve, overlook spatial information, despite the well-established observation that spatially adjacent spots often share similar cellular compositions. Motivated by this, we propose SpatialCD, a spatially
papers.summary: SpatialCD is a new computational method that improves cell-type deconvolution for spatial transcriptomics data without needing a reference single-cell RNA-seq dataset, by incorporating spatial information from neighboring spots. This approach outperforms existing reference-free methods by leveraging the fact that adjacent tissue spots tend to have similar cell-type compositions, leading to more accurate and biologically meaningful results.

papers.title: Condition-Associated Pattern Extraction and Recovery From Multi-Condition Single-Cell RNA-seq Data With CAPER.
papers.abstract: A central challenge in multi-condition single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data analysis is the disentanglement of true biological signals from unwanted variations in complex experimental designs. Current statistical and machine learning-based methods struggle with this task, often providing only visualizable embeddings, over-correcting and discarding biological signal, or failing to resolve cell-type-specific responses. Here, we present CAPER, a matrix factorization framework that explicitly disentangles shared biological states from condition-specific variations. CAPER directly outputs an
papers.summary: CAPER is a new computational framework that disentangles true biological signals from unwanted variations in multi-condition single-cell RNA-seq data, overcoming limitations of current methods that either over-correct or fail to capture cell-type-specific responses. By using matrix factorization, it explicitly separates shared biological states from condition-specific variations, enabling more accurate recovery of condition-associated patterns.

papers.title: Benchmarking cell type annotation in spatial transcriptomics: resolving cellular hierarchies, biological fidelity, and dynamic cell states.
papers.abstract: Spatial transcriptomics enables the quantification of gene expression within its native tissue context, providing unprecedented insight into tissue architecture, cellular ecosystems, and local cell-cell interactions at regional and single-cell resolution. Accurate cell type annotation is a critical prerequisite for interpreting these data and is often the first and most essential step in downstream analysis. Despite rapid advances in computational methods, cell type annotation remains challenging and frequently requires extensive expert-driven manual curation based on marker-gene expression, s
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This paper benchmarks computational methods for cell type annotation in spatial transcriptomics, focusing on their ability to resolve cellular hierarchies, biological fidelity, and dynamic cell states. The study highlights that accurate annotation remains challenging and often requires expert manual curation despite advances in computational tools.

papers.title: Thrombospondin-2 deficiency primes the synovial joint for aberrant tissue remodeling and injury response.
papers.abstract: This study investigates joint injury-induced angiogenesis and the effects of genetic deficiency of thrombospondin-2 (TSP2), an anti-angiogenic factor, in joint homeostasis and post-traumatic osteoarthritis (PTOA).
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study shows that mice lacking the anti-angiogenic factor thrombospondin-2 (TSP2) have abnormal joint structure and an exaggerated injury response, leading to more severe post-traumatic osteoarthritis. The findings suggest TSP2 is critical for maintaining normal joint tissue and preventing aberrant remodeling after injury.

papers.title: Comprehensive assessment of alternative splicing analysis methods for single-cell RNA-seq.
papers.abstract: Alternative splicing expands transcriptomic and proteomic diversity and contributes to cell identity and function. Advances in single-cell RNA sequencing enable transcriptome profiling at cellular resolution, yet their performance across biologically relevant tasks has not been systematically evaluated. Here, we benchmark six representative alternative splicing analysis methods across multiple full-length, short-read scRNA-seq datasets, focusing on biologically relevant tasks including cell clustering and differential splicing detection. Among these methods, scQuint achieves robust performance
papers.summary: This study benchmarks six methods for analyzing alternative splicing in single-cell RNA-seq data, finding that scQuint performs best for tasks like cell clustering and detecting differential splicing. The work provides guidance for researchers choosing tools to study splicing diversity at single-cell resolution.

papers.title: Interpretable multi-omics integration across mixed-order tensors with MANTRA.
papers.abstract: The integration of multi-modal molecular data is crucial for understanding complex diseases, but existing methods struggle with modern experimental designs that generate datasets with mixed-order tensors-for example, a third-order drug-response tensor alongside a second-order transcriptomics matrix. Here, we present MANTRA (Multi-view ANalysis with Tensor and matRix Alignment), a probabilistic framework that integrates collections of tensors of different orders, combining the strengths of group factor analysis and tensor decomposition. MANTRA learns interpretable latent factors and naturally h
papers.summary: MANTRA is a new computational framework that integrates multi-omics data of different dimensionalities (e.g., 3D drug-response tensors with 2D gene expression matrices) into a unified, interpretable model. It combines group factor analysis and tensor decomposition to learn latent factors that capture shared and dataset-specific variation, enabling better understanding of complex diseases.

papers.title: Annotation-Based Gene-Peak Links Improve Regulatory Network Prediction of Gene Expression in Human Kidney Multi-Omics.
papers.abstract: Linking distal regulatory elements to their target genes is a central problem for interpreting chromatin accessibility and other non-coding genomic data. Proximity-based mapping is convenient but ignores three-dimensional enhancer-promoter architecture and can misassign long-range regulatory effects. Correlation-based approaches can also miss regulatory links because of limited statistical power and restrictive distance or significance thresholds. Single-cell and single-nucleus multi-omic datasets, such as 10x Multiome profiles that jointly measure chromatin accessibility and gene expression i
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This paper presents a new annotation-based method for linking distal regulatory elements to their target genes, improving regulatory network prediction from single-cell multi-omic data. Applied to human kidney data, the approach outperforms proximity- and correlation-based methods by leveraging prior annotations to capture long-range regulatory interactions. This work provides a practical tool for interpreting non-coding genomic data in complex tissues.

papers.title: APLP2 as a molecular link between immune regulation and bone metabolism in hepatocellular carcinoma: evidence from scRNA-seq and functional validation.
papers.abstract: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) remains a significant health concern worldwide, characterized by elevated mortality rates that are often associated with diagnoses occurring in advanced stages and the restricted efficacy of treatment options currently available. Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) demonstrate promise in treating HCC; nonetheless, challenges related to therapeutic resistance and varied responses among patients underscore the necessity of identifying new biomarkers and comprehending the fundamental mechanisms involved. This research explores the molecular relationship between immu
papers.summary: This study identifies APLP2 as a molecular link connecting immune regulation and bone metabolism in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), using single-cell RNA sequencing and functional experiments. The findings suggest APLP2 could serve as a biomarker to improve understanding of treatment resistance and patient response variability to immune checkpoint inhibitors.

papers.title: BAGE: a Bayesian framework for age prediction based on PBMC gene expression data.
papers.abstract: Estimating chronological age from biological data is increasingly important for clinical and research studies of aging and age related diseases. In regards to transcriptomic data, it has been proven that chronological age prediction widely depends on sample type. While single-cell RNA-seq based clocks using PBMC single-cell data have been reported, to our knowledge no publicly available bulk RNA-seq age predictor has been trained specifically on peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs). To address this gap, we aggregated 16 publicly available PBMCs bulk transcriptomic datasets comprising 174
papers.summary: This paper presents BAGE, a Bayesian framework for predicting chronological age from bulk RNA-seq data of peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs). The authors aggregated 16 public datasets to train the model, addressing the lack of a PBMC-specific bulk transcriptomic age predictor.

papers.title: Scalable hypothalamic neuron differentiation from human pluripotent stem cells suitable for modeling metabolic disorders.
papers.abstract: The hypothalamus, composed of multiple nuclei, is essential for maintaining the body's homeostasis. Within the mediobasal hypothalamus, the arcuate nucleus (ARC) contains key neuronal populations, including appetite-suppressing pro-opiomelanocortin (POMC) neurons that regulate energy and glucose balance. Here, we present a chemically defined, scalable method for differentiating human pluripotent stem cells (hPSCs) into hypothalamic neurons enriched for POMC cells, compatible with robotic cell culture platforms for high-throughput use. Neuronal identity was validated by MERFISH single-cell tran
papers.summary: This paper presents a new, scalable method for turning human stem cells into hypothalamic neurons, specifically appetite-regulating POMC neurons, using a chemical recipe that works with robotic platforms for high-throughput studies. The approach enables large-scale production of these neurons, which could be used to model metabolic disorders like obesity and diabetes in a dish.

papers.title: Border-Associated Macrophages in CNS Health and Disease: A Comprehensive Review of Ontogeny, Heterogeneity, and Functional Plasticity at Neural Interfaces.
papers.abstract: Border-associated macrophages (BAMs) represent a specialized population of tissue-resident immune cells strategically positioned at the critical interfaces between the central nervous system (CNS) and peripheral circulation, including the meninges, choroid plexus, and perivascular spaces. As frontline sentinels of the neuroimmune system, BAMs perform essential functions in immune surveillance, barrier integrity maintenance, and homeostatic regulation, yet their unique biology and disease-associated roles remain incompletely characterized compared to parenchymal microglia. This review aims to s
papers.summary: This review comprehensively examines border-associated macrophages (BAMs), specialized immune cells located at CNS-periphery interfaces like the meninges and choroid plexus, highlighting their distinct origins, heterogeneity, and functional roles in health and disease. Unlike better-studied microglia, BAMs serve as frontline sentinels for immune surveillance and barrier maintenance, and the review underscores their underappreciated contributions to neuroimmune regulation and potential as therapeutic targets.

papers.title: FastGxC: Fast and powerful context-specific eQTL mapping in bulk and single-cell data.
papers.abstract: Context-specific expression quantitative trait loci (eQTLs) mediate genetic risk for complex diseases, but current methods limit their characterization and interpretation. We introduce FastGxC, a method for efficiently mapping context-specific eQTLs by leveraging correlation structure in multi-tissue bulk and single-cell RNA sequencing studies. In simulations, FastGxC is nine times more powerful and 106 times faster than existing approaches, reducing computation time from years to minutes. We applied FastGxC to bulk multi-tissue (n = 698) and peripheral blood mononuclear cell (PBMC) single-cel
papers.summary: FastGxC is a new computational method that maps context-specific eQTLs much faster and with greater statistical power than existing tools, reducing computation from years to minutes. It works by leveraging correlation structures in both bulk multi-tissue and single-cell RNA sequencing data, enabling researchers to better understand how genetic variants influence gene expression in specific cell types or conditions.

papers.title: CAdir: Joint clustering of cells and genes for single-cell transcriptomics with visualization-driven cluster quality assessment.
papers.abstract: Clustering for single-cell RNA-seq aims at finding similar cells and grouping them into biologically meaningful clusters. Many available clustering algorithms however do not not provide the cluster defining marker genes or are unable to infer the number of clusters in an unsupervised manner as well as lack tools to easily determine the quality of the label assignments. Therefore, clustering quality is commonly evaluated by visually inspecting low-dimensional embeddings as produced by, e.g., UMAP or t-SNE. These embeddings can, however, distort the true cluster structure and are known to produc
papers.summary: CAdir is a new method for single-cell RNA-seq clustering that jointly clusters cells and genes, automatically determines the number of clusters, and provides a visualization-driven tool to assess cluster quality. This addresses common limitations of existing algorithms that rely on potentially misleading low-dimensional embeddings like UMAP for quality evaluation.

papers.title: Multilevel brain heart interaction correlates with suicidal ideation in first episode drug naive major depressive disorder.
papers.abstract: Emerging evidence highlights the critical role of brain-heart interaction (BHI) in regulating affective states. However, BHI alterations and their molecular underpinnings in first-episode drug-naïve (FeDn) major depressive disorder (MDD) patients with suicidal ideation (SI) remain unclear. We collected synchronized electroencephalogram (EEG) and electrocardiogram (ECG) data from 81 MDD patients with SI (MDD-SI), 55 without SI (MDD-NSI), and 82 healthy controls (HCs). An integrative framework was developed to link a multilevel EEG-ECG derived BHI index with regional gene expression. Our study r
papers.summary: This study found that brain-heart interaction (BHI) patterns, measured via synchronized EEG and ECG, are altered in first-episode drug-naïve major depressive disorder patients with suicidal ideation compared to those without and healthy controls. The researchers developed a framework linking these BHI changes to regional gene expression, suggesting a biological basis for suicidal ideation in depression.

papers.title: Spatial transcriptomic profiling of ovarian clear cell carcinoma reveals heterogeneity in OXPHOS and EMT gradients.
papers.abstract: Intratumoral heterogeneity is intrinsically comprised of molecular alterations of tumor cells and extrinsically from interconnections with microenvironments. This study explores the spatial heterogeneity of ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC), a rare cancer with significance to East Asian women. We profile 21 primary-metastatic tumor pairs in a discovery cohort and 16 tumors in two validation cohorts using spatial transcriptomic (ST) platforms. Our integrative analysis revealed an inverse relationship between OXPHOS and inflammation along the EMT gradient. OCCC cells undergoing partial EMT hav
papers.summary: This study uses spatial transcriptomics to map heterogeneity in ovarian clear cell carcinoma, revealing an inverse relationship between oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) and inflammation along the epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) gradient. The findings highlight how tumor cells undergoing partial EMT exhibit distinct metabolic and inflammatory states, providing insights into the spatial organization of this rare cancer.

papers.title: The extracellular matrix in cancer-associated fibrosis: molecular mechanisms and clinical relevance.
papers.abstract: The ECM is a dynamic component of the tumor microenvironment with a critical role in cancer progression, invasion, metastasis, immune exclusion, and response to therapy. Recent advances in proteomic analyses investigating the insoluble ECM fractions (termed "matrisome analysis"), along with single-cell RNA sequencing and spatial transcriptomics, have revealed cancer-specific patterns of ECM remodeling. These studies have identified a panel of recurrently upregulated ECM proteins, including annexin A1, fibrillin-1, fibronectin, periostin, and tenascin-C, actively contributing to tumor growth, i
papers.summary: This review discusses how the extracellular matrix (ECM) in the tumor microenvironment drives cancer progression, invasion, and therapy resistance, and highlights recent advances in matrisome analysis, single-cell RNA sequencing, and spatial transcriptomics that have identified key ECM proteins (e.g., annexin A1, fibrillin-1) as potential therapeutic targets.

papers.title: Age-associated niche remodeling drives dental pulp progenitor dysfunction and fibrosis.
papers.abstract: Dental pulp homeostasis and regeneration rely on specialized mesenchymal populations, yet aging promotes fibrotic remodeling and functional decline. Here, using the continuously growing mouse incisor as an experimentally tractable model, we integrate single-cell RNA sequencing, spatial transcriptomics, lineage tracing, and functional assays to investigate age-associated pulp remodeling. We identify a distinct Lypd1+ fibroblast progenitor population that is particularly vulnerable to age-associated dysfunction. Lineage tracing and trajectory analysis show that these cells originate from the Sfr
papers.summary: This study uses single-cell and spatial transcriptomics in mouse incisors to show that aging drives fibrosis and functional decline in dental pulp by disrupting a specific Lypd1+ fibroblast progenitor population. The findings reveal how age-associated niche remodeling impairs regeneration and may inform future therapies for dental repair.

papers.title: Primary sclerosing cholangitis displays distinct colonic mucosa topography yet a shared mast cell state with ulcerative colitis.
papers.abstract: Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) is a chronic, progressing cholestatic disease that often co-occurs with inflammatory bowel disease (PSC-IBD). PSC-IBD affecting the colon (PSC-ulcerative colitis or PSC-UC) resembles clinical UC, but is characterised by less severe disease flares, right-colon predominance, and a greater lifetime risk of colorectal cancer than UC alone. To elucidate differences in the underlying biology between PSC-UC and UC, here we combine single-cell mRNA and antigen receptor sequencing, 16S ribosomal RNA gene analysis and spatial transcriptomics on biopsies from four col
papers.summary: This study combines single-cell sequencing, microbiome analysis, and spatial transcriptomics to compare colonic biopsies from patients with primary sclerosing cholangitis-associated ulcerative colitis (PSC-UC) and UC alone. It finds that PSC-UC has a distinct right-colon topography and a unique mast cell state shared with UC, suggesting common inflammatory pathways despite different disease presentations.

papers.title: Single-cell and spatial transcriptomics map lung-adaptive metastasis programs through Exoc4, Cd36-vasculogenic mimicry, and Cxcr2 signaling in triple-negative breast cancer.
papers.abstract: Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) is an aggressive subtype associated with high metastatic potential and poor outcomes, with lung metastasis representing one of the most frequent and life-threatening events. However, the mechanism how cellular plasticity and reprogramming that enable TNBC cells to colonize in the lung remain incompletely understood. Here, we integrated single-cell RNA sequencing and spatial transcriptomics on paired MMTV-PyMT primary mammary tumors and lung metastatic tumors, complemented by functional assays in 4T1 mouse models, to delineate the cellular heterogeneity and
papers.summary: This study uses single-cell and spatial transcriptomics to map how triple-negative breast cancer cells adapt to colonize the lung, identifying key programs involving Exoc4, Cd36-mediated vasculogenic mimicry, and Cxcr2 signaling. These findings reveal cellular plasticity mechanisms that drive lung metastasis, offering potential therapeutic targets for this aggressive cancer.

papers.title: Spatial transcriptome and single-cell reveal the role of sorbitol metabolism in hepatocellular carcinoma progression and tumor microenvironment.
papers.abstract: Metabolic reprogramming represents a hallmark feature of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). As a crucial branch of the polyol pathway, the biological functions and clinical implications of sorbitol metabolism in HCC progression remain to be fully elucidated.
papers.summary: This study uses spatial transcriptomics and single-cell RNA sequencing to investigate sorbitol metabolism in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). The authors find that sorbitol metabolism is reprogrammed in HCC and plays a role in tumor progression and shaping the tumor microenvironment. These findings suggest that targeting sorbitol metabolism could be a potential therapeutic strategy for HCC.

papers.title: Mapping brain-wide monosynaptic inputs to single neurons with ROInet-seq.
papers.abstract: Viral projection tracing strategies help build regional connectomes of mammalian brains. G-deleted rabies virus (ΔG-RV) establishes monosynaptic input connectivity but cannot distinguish networks at cell resolution. Here, we implement a barcoded ΔG-RV for network tracing by quantitative RNA-sequencing. At optimized library complexity and uniformity, barcode detection reliably distinguished individual monosynaptic input networks of multiple infected neurons in parallel. To scale this approach to hundreds of cells and full-brain inputs, we develop regions-of-interest network sequencing (ROInet-s
papers.summary: This paper introduces ROInet-seq, a method that uses barcoded rabies virus and RNA sequencing to map the monosynaptic inputs to many individual neurons across the entire brain simultaneously. This approach overcomes the limitation of traditional viral tracing, which cannot distinguish inputs to single cells, enabling high-resolution connectomics at cellular scale.

papers.title: A peripheral subpopulation of retinal pigment epithelium resists oxidative damage through SERPINE3-mediated Caspase-1 inhibition.
papers.abstract: Heterogeneous degeneration of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) leads to irreversible blindness in diseases associated with macular atrophy. However, the underlying mechanisms of regional RPE degeneration remain poorly understood. To address this gap, this study identifies a peripheral RPE subpopulation through spatial, transcriptomic, and functional analyses, thereby contributing to the understanding of the heterogeneity of degenerative RPE cells. Specifically, omics analyses in human and macaque RPE reveal a peripheral RPE cell population with high SERPINE3 expression, while SERPINE3-GFP
papers.summary: This study identifies a specific subpopulation of retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) cells in the periphery that resists oxidative damage by expressing SERPINE3, which inhibits Caspase-1. Using spatial and transcriptomic analyses in human and macaque eyes, the researchers show that this peripheral RPE population is protected from degeneration, offering new insights into why macular atrophy occurs in central vision areas. The finding highlights SERPINE3 as a potential therapeutic target for preventing RPE degeneration in blinding diseases.

papers.title: The human LRRK2-R1441G mutation drives age-dependent oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction in dopaminergic neurons.
papers.abstract: Mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress are central to the pathogenesis of Parkinson's disease (PD), particularly affecting substantia nigra pars compacta (SNc) dopamine (DA) neurons. Here, we investigate how the R1441G mutation in leucine-rich repeat kinase 2 (LRRK2), a key genetic contributor to familial and sporadic PD, impacts mitochondrial function in midbrain DA neurons.
papers.summary: This study shows that the LRRK2-R1441G mutation, a genetic cause of Parkinson's disease, leads to age-dependent oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction specifically in dopamine neurons. The findings provide mechanistic insight into how this mutation contributes to neuronal vulnerability in Parkinson's disease.

papers.title: Spatial transcriptomic profiling reveals molecular signatures of endocapillary hypercellularity in IgA nephropathy.
papers.abstract: Immunoglobulin A nephropathy (IgAN) is a leading cause of chronic kidney disease, marked by mesangial IgA1 immune-complex deposition and a multi-hit pathogenesis. The Oxford classification grades histopathological lesions such as endocapillary hypercellularity (E0 and E1), which are associated with disease severity and prognosis. Despite advances in single-cell and spatial transcriptomics, the molecular basis of endocapillary hypercellularity in human IgAN remains unclear.
papers.summary: This study uses spatial transcriptomics to identify molecular signatures associated with endocapillary hypercellularity (E1 lesion) in IgA nephropathy, revealing distinct gene expression patterns that may underlie this pathological feature. The findings provide new insights into the molecular basis of a key histopathological lesion linked to disease severity and prognosis.

papers.title: Multiscale systems modelling of communication networks in brain metastasis.
papers.abstract: Brain metastasis remains a major clinical challenge because intracranial progression and therapeutic resistance arise not only from tumour-intrinsic programmes but also from coordinated communication across molecular, cellular, spatial and systemic scales. In this Review, we integrate findings from spatial transcriptomics/proteomics, single-cell and multimodal omics, and computational systems modelling to frame brain metastasis as a multiscale communication disorder in which immune, glial, vascular and systemic networks collectively shape tumour-permissive ecosystems. We discuss how network-ba
papers.summary: This review frames brain metastasis as a multiscale communication disorder, integrating spatial transcriptomics, single-cell omics, and computational systems modeling to show how immune, glial, vascular, and systemic networks collectively create tumor-permissive ecosystems. It highlights the potential of network-based approaches to identify new therapeutic targets that disrupt these coordinated communication networks.

papers.title: Granzyme K CD8⁺ T cells with tissue-resident features promote intestinal inflammation in patients with Crohn's disease.
papers.abstract: The role of CD8+ T cells in Crohn's disease (CD) pathogenesis remains incompletely understood. This study aimed to characterize CD8+ T cells in CD and elucidate their potential contribution to intestinal inflammation. T cells from blood and intestinal tissues of 15 patients with CD were analyzed using single-cell RNA and T cell receptor sequencing. Spatial transcriptomics was conducted on inflamed intestinal tissues from two patients. Analysis of 41,699 CD8+ T cells identified distinct subsets characterized by differential granzyme expression: granzyme B (GZMB+) CD8+ T cells, predominantly in
papers.summary: This study identifies a specific subset of CD8+ T cells expressing granzyme K (GZMK) with tissue-resident features that promote intestinal inflammation in Crohn's disease patients. Using single-cell RNA sequencing and spatial transcriptomics, the authors show these GZMK+ cells are enriched in inflamed gut tissue and likely contribute to disease pathology. The findings provide new insights into the role of CD8+ T cells in Crohn's disease and suggest GZMK as a potential therapeutic target.

papers.title: Single-cell spatial landscape of aggrephagy activity stratifies hepatocellular carcinoma neutrophils and delivers a 5-gene diagnostic panel for patient stratification.
papers.abstract: Hepatocellular carcinoma (LIHC) features a complex tumor microenvironment (TME) where tumor-associated neutrophils (TANs) show significant plasticity. The role of aggrephagy-selective autophagy of protein aggregates-in shaping neutrophil heterogeneity and LIHC progression remains poorly understood.
papers.summary: This study maps aggrephagy activity in hepatocellular carcinoma at single-cell resolution, revealing that it drives neutrophil heterogeneity and tumor progression. The authors developed a 5-gene diagnostic panel based on aggrephagy-related neutrophil states that can stratify patients. This provides a new tool for prognosis and potential therapeutic targeting in liver cancer.

papers.title: PLNFGL: Joint Estimation of Multi-Condition Gene Networks from Single-cell RNA-seq Data.
papers.abstract: Graphical models have been widely used in bioinformatics to infer the conditional dependence structure among random variables, but traditional Gaussian graphical models (GGMs) are suboptimal for single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) due to dropout events and distributional mismatch. Moreover, most existing methods estimate networks under a single condition, limiting their utility in multi-condition studies.
papers.summary: This paper introduces PLNFGL, a method that jointly estimates gene regulatory networks across multiple conditions from single-cell RNA-seq data, addressing challenges like dropout events and distributional mismatch that make traditional Gaussian graphical models unsuitable. By leveraging information across conditions, it improves network inference and enables comparative analysis of gene networks in different biological contexts.

papers.title: SpaHNR: a spatial domain identification method via sparse attention-based hierarchical node representation and multi-view contrastive learning.
papers.abstract: Leveraging deep learning on spatial transcriptomics data enables the identification of distinct spatial domains within tissues, thereby clarifies the spatial organization of cells and their gene expression. This detailed spatial understanding is crucial for dissecting intricate biological processes and the mechanisms of disease. However, existing computational approaches often analyze cell or spot (representing a small number of cells) adjacency relationships at a single level of detail (resolution), overlooking the natural hierarchical organization inherent in biological tissues.
papers.summary: SpaHNR is a new deep learning method that identifies spatial domains in tissues by analyzing gene expression data at multiple levels of detail, capturing the natural hierarchical organization of cells. It uses sparse attention and multi-view contrastive learning to improve accuracy over existing single-resolution approaches. This could help researchers better understand tissue architecture and disease mechanisms.

papers.title: Integrated single-cell and spatial transcriptomics reveal stromal-immune-vascular crosstalk in patients with interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome.
papers.abstract: Interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS) is characterized by complex cellular heterogeneity and inflammatory dysfunction in the bladder. However, the molecular pathogenesis of IC/BPS remains poorly understood, and few studies have provided comprehensive analyses. To elucidate the mechanisms underlying disease pathology, we performed integrated single-cell and spatial transcriptomic analyses of human bladder tissue. Comprehensive single-cell multiomics analyses have identified disease-specific alterations across multiple cell types. T helper 17 cells were significantly expanded in H
papers.summary: This study uses integrated single-cell and spatial transcriptomics to map the cellular landscape of the bladder in interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS), revealing disease-specific alterations and expansion of T helper 17 cells. The findings highlight complex stromal-immune-vascular crosstalk that may drive inflammation and pathology in IC/BPS.

papers.title: Spatial transcriptomic atlas of aggressive osteosarcomas reveals shared immune landscape and targetable surface markers.
papers.abstract: Osteosarcoma is characterized by extensive inter- and intra-tumoral heterogeneity, contributing to treatment resistance and poor outcomes. Here, we present a comprehensive spatial transcriptomics analysis of osteosarcoma, encompassing primary tumors and local or metastatic relapses across diverse phenotypic subtypes. Despite this heterogeneity, we identify a nine-gene cell surface signature with theranostic potential, validated in independent datasets and shown by immunohistochemistry to be distributed across distinct tumor compartments, supporting multi-targeted therapeutic strategies. Analys
papers.summary: This study uses spatial transcriptomics to map the cellular landscape of aggressive osteosarcomas, revealing a shared immune environment despite high tumor heterogeneity. The authors identify a nine-gene cell surface signature with potential for both diagnosis and therapy, validated across independent datasets and tumor compartments. This work suggests that multi-targeted therapeutic strategies may be effective against this heterogeneous cancer.

papers.title: MCFST: Spatial domain identification method based on multi-view graph convolutional network and graph fusion network.
papers.abstract: The emergence of spatial transcriptomics, which integrates spatial and gene expression information, has greatly advanced research in disease mechanisms and developmental biology. A core task in this field is spatial domain identification, which reveals regions with shared molecular signatures and histological features, thereby facilitating the study of tissue function and pathology. Although existing methods have achieved promising performance, many of them still face limitations in effectively integrating heterogeneous information from multiple views, such as gene expression, spatial coordina
papers.summary: This paper presents MCFST, a new computational method that uses a multi-view graph convolutional network and graph fusion network to better identify spatial domains in spatial transcriptomics data. By integrating heterogeneous information from gene expression and spatial coordinates, MCFST improves the accuracy of identifying tissue regions with shared molecular and histological features.

papers.title: Identifying condition-related cell-cell communication events using supervised tensor analysis.
papers.abstract: Many tools have been developed to infer active cell-cell communication (CCC) events, which are essential for understanding biological processes and diseases. However, existing methods for assessing the relationships between CCC events and biological conditions have at least one practical limitation: a lack of clear interpretation, an inability to adjust for confounders, or an inability to model inherent dependencies among CCC events. To comprehensively address these limitations, we introduce STACCato, a supervised tensor analysis tool for identifying condition-related CCC events. STACCato empl
papers.summary: This paper introduces STACCato, a new tool that uses supervised tensor analysis to identify cell-cell communication events related to specific biological conditions. Unlike existing methods, STACCato addresses key limitations by providing clear interpretation, adjusting for confounders, and modeling dependencies among communication events.

papers.title: A pan-cancer single-cell atlas uncovers the role of sex hormones and chromosomes in sex-divergent reprogramming of the tumor microenvironment.
papers.abstract: Sex bias is pervasive in tumors; however, how sex chromosomes and hormone-responsive signaling shape the tumor microenvironment (TME) remains insufficiently characterized. Considering the critical impact of the TME on tumor progression and response to immunotherapy, a pan-cancer investigation of sex-specific and cancer-context-dependent TME features is warranted.
papers.summary: This study creates a pan-cancer single-cell atlas to investigate how sex hormones and chromosomes contribute to differences in the tumor microenvironment between males and females. The findings reveal sex-divergent reprogramming of the TME, which may influence tumor progression and immunotherapy responses.
